content:
# Numbers Chapter 17 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 169-170 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_017_page_001.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0125-0126

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE AFTERMATH OF KORAH AND THE BUDDING OF AARON'S ROD:** Numbers 17 in the Hebrew Masoretic tradition contains twenty-eight verses and divides into three distinct movements: the sacred disposal of the fire-pans of the dead incense-offerers and their transformation into altar plating (vv. 1-5); the plague that strikes the congregation when they murmur against Moses and Aaron, and Aaron's dramatic intercession with the censer standing between the living and the dead (vv. 6-15); and the sign of Aaron's rod that buds, blossoms, and bears almonds — YHWH's definitive confirmation of the Aaronic priesthood (vv. 16-28). The Leningrad Codex presents this chapter across pages 169-170 (folios 85r-85v), continuing directly from the conclusion of chapter 16. The Vaticanus manuscript (pa_0125-0126) preserves the LXX text under a different chapter division: the fire-pan episode appears as the conclusion of LXX chapter 16 (= chapter XIX-XX in the Greek), and the rod narrative as LXX chapter 17 (= chapter XVII in older Greek numbering). English Bibles vary significantly in versification: many English translations number vv. 1-15 of this Hebrew chapter as 16:36-50, and vv. 16-28 as 17:1-13. The EMET translation follows the Masoretic verse numbering throughout. The chapter forms a theological unit: the fire-pans become a memorial warning (vv. 1-5), the plague demonstrates the lethal danger of approaching YHWH without authorization (vv. 6-15), and the budding rod provides a permanent, living sign that YHWH has chosen Aaron (vv. 16-28). The rod episode is one of the most striking miracles in the Torah — a dead stick of wood sprouts, flowers, and produces ripe fruit overnight, reversing the natural order of life and death as a sign of divine election. The almond (שָׁקֵד, shaqed) carries additional resonance: the root שׁקד means "to watch, be wakeful," and the almond tree is the first to blossom in spring — the "watchful tree" (cf. Jer 1:11-12). YHWH is "watching" over His word and over His chosen priest.

---

## The Fire-Pans of the Dead: A Memorial (vv. 1-5)

**1** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor) = "and YHWH spoke to Moses, saying" — the standard prophetic speech formula. The piel vav-consecutive of דבר (davar, "to speak"). YHWH now addresses the aftermath of the fire that consumed the two hundred fifty men (16:35). **LXX (17:1 = 16:36 in some Greek MSS):** καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων ("and the Lord spoke to Moses, saying"). **Versification note:** This verse corresponds to Numbers 16:36 [Eng. 17:1] in many English translations. The Hebrew Masoretic tradition begins chapter 17 here; the EMET follows the MT chapter division.

**2** "Say [emor] unto [el] Eleazar [El'azar] son of [ben] Aaron [Aharon] the-priest [ha-kohen], and-let-him-lift-up [ve-yarem] the fire-pans [et ha-makhtot] from-between [mi-bein] the-burning [ha-sereifah], and-the-fire [ve-et ha-esh] scatter [zereh] yonder [hal'ah], for [ki] they-have-become-holy [qadoshu] —

> **Key terms:** אֱמֹר אֶל־אֶלְעָזָר בֶּן־אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֵן (emor el El'azar ben Aharon ha-kohen) = "say to Eleazar son of Aaron the priest" — Eleazar (not Aaron) is sent to retrieve the fire-pans, perhaps because Aaron as high priest must not defile himself among the dead (Lev 21:11), or because this task is beneath the high-priestly office. וְיָרֵם אֶת־הַמַּחְתֹּת מִבֵּין הַשְּׂרֵפָה (ve-yarem et ha-makhtot mi-bein ha-sereifah) = "and let him lift up the fire-pans from between the burning" — the hifil jussive of רום (rum, "to be high/lift up"). הַשְּׂרֵפָה (ha-sereifah, "the burning") from שׂרף (saraf, "to burn") — the smoldering remains of the divine fire that consumed the rebels. וְאֶת־הָאֵשׁ זְרֵה־הַלְאָה (ve-et ha-esh zereh hal'ah) = "and the fire scatter yonder" — the qal imperative of זרה (zarah, "to scatter/winnow") + הַלְאָה (hal'ah, "further, yonder"). The sacred fire in the pans must be scattered at a distance. כִּי קָדֵשׁוּ (ki qadoshu) = "for they have become holy" — the qal perfect 3cp of קדשׁ (qadash, "to be holy"). The fire-pans, though used in unauthorized worship, became holy through their contact with YHWH's altar-fire. Holiness, once conferred, cannot be undone — even objects used in sin retain their consecrated status. **LXX:** ὅτι ἡγίασται ("for they have been sanctified") — the passive voice making explicit what the Hebrew stative implies.

**3** the fire-pans of [et makhtot] these [ha-elleh] sinners [ha-khatta'im] against-their-souls [be-nafshosam], and-they-shall-make [ve-asu] them [otam] hammered-plates [riqqulei] of-plating [pakhim] for-the-altar [la-mizbeakh], for [ki] they-brought-them-near [hiqrivum] before [lifnei] YHWH, and-they-became-holy [va-yiqdoshu]; and-they-shall-be [ve-yihyu] for-a-sign [le-ot] for-the-Sons of [li-vnei] Israel [Yisra'el]."

> **Key terms:** אֵת מַחְתּוֹת הַחַטָּאִים הָאֵלֶּה בְּנַפְשֹׁתָם (et makhtot ha-khatta'im ha-elleh be-nafshosam) = "the fire-pans of these sinners against their souls" — חַטָּאִים (khatta'im, "sinners") from חטא (khata, "to sin/miss the mark"). The phrase בְּנַפְשֹׁתָם (be-nafshosam, "against their souls/lives") indicates that their sin cost them their lives — they sinned at the price of their own existence. וְעָשׂוּ אֹתָם רִקֻּעֵי פַחִים (ve-asu otam riqqulei fakhim) = "and they shall make them hammered plates of plating" — רִקֻּעֵי (riqqulei) from רקע (raqa, "to beat out, stamp, spread"), the same root describing the רָקִיעַ (raqia, "firmament/expanse") beaten out at creation (Gen 1:6-7). פַּחִים (fakhim, "plates/sheets") — thin metal sheeting. The fire-pans are to be hammered flat and applied as plating to the bronze altar. צִפּוּי לַמִּזְבֵּחַ (tsippui la-mizbeakh) = "a plating for the altar" — from צפה (tsafah, "to plate/overlay"). כִּי־הִקְרִיבֻם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וַיִּקְדָּשׁוּ (ki hiqrivum lifnei YHWH va-yiqdoshu) = "for they brought them near before YHWH, and they became holy" — the hifil of קרב (qarav, "to draw near") + qal of קדשׁ. The theological principle: objects presented before YHWH are irrevocably consecrated. וְיִהְיוּ לְאוֹת לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (ve-yihyu le-ot li-vnei Yisra'el) = "and they shall be for a sign for the Sons of Israel" — אוֹת (ot, "sign") — a visible, perpetual reminder hammered into the altar itself. **LXX:** ὅτι προσήνεγκαν αὐτὰ ἔναντι κυρίου καὶ ἡγιάσθησαν· καὶ ἐγένοντο εἰς σημεῖον τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ("for they brought them before the Lord, and they were sanctified; and they became a sign for the sons of Israel").

**4** And-took [va-yiqqakh] Eleazar [El'azar] the-priest [ha-kohen] the fire-pans of [et makhtot] the-bronze [ha-nekhoshet] which [asher] had-brought-near [hiqrivu] the-ones-who-were-burned [ha-seru'fim], and-they-hammered-them-out [va-yeraqqu'um] as-plating [tsippui] for-the-altar [la-mizbeakh] —

> **Key terms:** וַיִּקַּח אֶלְעָזָר הַכֹּהֵן אֵת מַחְתּוֹת הַנְּחֹשֶׁת אֲשֶׁר הִקְרִיבוּ הַשְּׂרֻפִים (va-yiqqakh El'azar ha-kohen et makhtot ha-nekhoshet asher hiqrivu ha-seru'fim) = "and Eleazar the priest took the fire-pans of bronze which the ones-who-were-burned had brought near" — הַשְּׂרֻפִים (ha-seru'fim, "the burned ones") — the qal passive participle of שׂרף (saraf, "to burn"), used substantively to describe the two hundred fifty men consumed by divine fire. The word is morphologically identical to שְׂרָפִים (serafim, "burning ones" — the angelic beings of Isaiah 6:2), though here the "burning" is punitive rather than glorious. וַיְרַקְּעוּם צִפּוּי לַמִּזְבֵּחַ (va-yeraqqu'um tsippui la-mizbeakh) = "and they hammered them out as plating for the altar" — the piel vav-consecutive of רקע (raqa, "to beat out, hammer") + 3mp suffix. **LXX:** καὶ ἔλαβεν Ελεαζαρ υἱὸς Ααρων τοῦ ἱερέως τὰ πυρεῖα τὰ χαλκᾶ ("and Eleazar son of Aaron the priest took the bronze censers") — note the LXX specifies "son of Aaron" where the MT has simply "the priest."

**5** a memorial [zikkaron] for-the-Sons of [li-vnei] Israel [Yisra'el], so-that [le-ma'an] not [lo] shall-come-near [yiqrav] a-man [ish] who-is-a-stranger [zar], who [asher] [is] not [lo] of-the-seed of [mi-zera] Aaron [Aharon], he [hu], to-burn [le-haqtir] incense [qetoret] before [lifnei] YHWH, and-not [ve-lo] shall-he-be [yihyeh] like-Korah [ke-Qorakh] and-like-his-congregation [ve-kha-adato], as [ka-asher] spoke [dibber] YHWH by-the-hand of [be-yad] Moses [Mosheh] to-him [lo].

> **Key terms:** זִכָּרוֹן לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (zikkaron li-vnei Yisra'el) = "a memorial for the Sons of Israel" — from זכר (zakhar, "to remember"). The hammered altar-plating serves as a perpetual physical reminder. לְמַעַן לֹא־יִקְרַב אִישׁ זָר (le-ma'an lo yiqrav ish zar) = "so that no stranger shall come near" — זָר (zar, "stranger/outsider/unauthorized person") — anyone not of the Aaronic priestly line. The root זור (zur) indicates alienness from a particular status or sphere. אֲשֶׁר לֹא מִזֶּרַע אַהֲרֹן הוּא (asher lo mi-zera Aharon hu) = "who is not of the seed of Aaron, he" — the emphatic pronoun הוּא (hu, "he") underscores the exclusivity. לְהַקְטִיר קְטֹרֶת (le-haqtir qetoret) = "to burn incense" — the hifil infinitive of קטר (qatar, "to send up smoke, burn incense"), cognate with קְטֹרֶת (qetoret, "incense"). Only the sons of Aaron may offer incense. וְלֹא־יִהְיֶה כְקֹרַח וְכַעֲדָתוֹ (ve-lo yihyeh ke-Qorakh ve-kha-adato) = "and he shall not be like Korah and like his congregation" — a warning by negative example. כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה בְּיַד־מֹשֶׁה לוֹ (ka-asher dibber YHWH be-yad Mosheh lo) = "as YHWH spoke by the hand of Moses to him" — the concluding formula. The antecedent of "to him" (לוֹ, lo) is ambiguous — it could refer to Eleazar, to the stranger, or to Korah. **LXX:** μνημόσυνον τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ, ὅπως ἂν μὴ προσέλθῃ μηθεὶς ἀλλογενής, ὃς οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος Ααρων ("a memorial for the sons of Israel, so that no one of another race, who is not of the seed of Aaron, should come near") — ἀλλογενής ("of another race/foreign-born") for zar, a stronger exclusionary term.

---

## The Plague and Aaron's Intercession (vv. 6-15)

**6** And-murmured [va-yillonu] all [kol] the-congregation of [adat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] on-the-next-day [mi-mokhorat] against [al] Moses [Mosheh] and-against [ve-al] Aaron [Aharon], saying [le'mor], "You [attem] have-killed [hamittem] the-people of [et am] YHWH."

> **Key terms:** וַיִּלֹּנוּ כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִמָּחֳרָת (va-yillonu kol adat benei Yisra'el mi-mokhorat) = "and all the congregation of the Sons of Israel murmured on the next day" — the niphal vav-consecutive of לון (lun, "to murmur/grumble"), the characteristic verb of the wilderness rebellion cycle. מִמָּחֳרָת (mi-mokhorat, "on the morrow, the next day") — astonishingly, the very day after witnessing the earth swallow the rebels and fire consume the incense-offerers, the people murmur again. אַתֶּם הֲמִתֶּם אֶת־עַם יְהוָה (attem hamittem et am YHWH) = "you have killed the people of YHWH" — the hifil perfect 2mp of מות (mut, "to die") — "you caused to die." The accusation is breathtaking: the congregation blames Moses and Aaron for the deaths that YHWH Himself inflicted. They call the dead rebels עַם יְהוָה (am YHWH, "the people of YHWH"), claiming divine-people status for those whom YHWH had just judged. **LXX:** καὶ ἐγόγγυσαν πᾶσα συναγωγὴ υἱῶν Ισραηλ τῇ ἐπαύριον ἐπὶ Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων λέγοντες Ὑμεῖς ἀπεκτάγκατε τὸν λαὸν κυρίου ("and the whole congregation of the sons of Israel murmured on the morrow against Moses and Aaron, saying, 'You have killed the people of the Lord'").

**7** And-it-was [va-yehi] when-assembled [be-hiqqahel] the-congregation [ha-edah] against [al] Moses [Mosheh] and-against [ve-al] Aaron [Aharon], and-they-turned [va-yifnu] unto [el] the-Tent of [ohel] Meeting [mo'ed], and-behold [ve-hinneh], covered-it [kissahu] the-cloud [he-anan], and-appeared [va-yera] the-glory of [kevod] YHWH.

> **Key terms:** וַיְהִי בְּהִקָּהֵל הָעֵדָה עַל־מֹשֶׁה וְעַל־אַהֲרֹן (va-yehi be-hiqqahel ha-edah al Mosheh ve-al Aharon) = "and it was, when the congregation assembled against Moses and against Aaron" — the niphal infinitive construct of קהל (qahal, "to assemble") with the hostile preposition עַל (al, "against"), the same verb used of the Korahite assembly in 16:3. וַיִּפְנוּ אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (va-yifnu el ohel mo'ed) = "and they turned to the Tent of Meeting" — the qal vav-consecutive of פנה (panah, "to turn"). The congregation turns toward the Tent, perhaps to confront Moses and Aaron there, or perhaps drawn by what they see. וְהִנֵּה כִסָּהוּ הֶעָנָן (ve-hinneh kissahu he-anan) = "and behold, the cloud covered it" — the dramatic הִנֵּה (hinneh, "behold!") signals sudden, unexpected divine manifestation. כִּסָּהוּ (kissahu) = piel of כסה (kasah, "to cover") + 3ms suffix — "it covered it." The cloud of YHWH's presence descends upon the Tent. וַיֵּרָא כְּבוֹד יְהוָה (va-yera kevod YHWH) = "and the glory of YHWH appeared" — the niphal of ראה (ra'ah, "to see") — the כָּבוֹד (kavod, "glory, weight, honor") of YHWH becomes visible. This is not a response to prayer but a spontaneous theophany provoked by the people's murmuring — and it portends judgment, not blessing. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπέβλεψαν ἐπὶ τὴν σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου, καὶ τήνδε ἐκάλυψεν αὐτὴν ἡ νεφέλη, καὶ ὤφθη ἡ δόξα κυρίου ("and they looked upon the tent of witness, and behold, the cloud covered it, and the glory of the Lord appeared") — σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("tent of the testimony/witness") for ohel mo'ed ("tent of meeting") — the LXX's standard divergent rendering.

**8** And-came [va-yavo] Moses [Mosheh] and-Aaron [ve-Aharon] unto [el] the-face of [penei] the-Tent of [ohel] Meeting [mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן אֶל־פְּנֵי אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (va-yavo Mosheh ve-Aharon el penei ohel mo'ed) = "and Moses and Aaron came to the face of the Tent of Meeting" — the qal vav-consecutive of בוא (bo, "to come/enter"). The singular verb form וַיָּבֹא (va-yavo) with a compound subject is common in biblical Hebrew, the verb agreeing with the first (and more prominent) subject. פְּנֵי (penei, "face of") indicates the front entrance of the Tent. **LXX:** καὶ εἰσῆλθεν Μωυσῆς καὶ Ααρων κατὰ πρόσωπον τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and Moses and Aaron entered before the face of the tent of the testimony").

**9** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor) = "and YHWH spoke to Moses, saying" — the same formula as v. 1. What follows is a command of judgment.

**10** "Get-away [herommu] from-the-midst of [mi-tokh] this [ha-zot] congregation [ha-edah], and-I-will-consume [va-akhaleh] them [otam] in-a-moment [ke-rega]." And-they-fell [va-yippelu] upon [al] their-faces [peneihem].

> **Key terms:** הֵרֹמּוּ מִתּוֹךְ הָעֵדָה הַזֹּאת (herommu mi-tokh ha-edah ha-zot) = "get away from the midst of this congregation" — the niphal imperative plural of רום (rum, "to be high/rise"), used here in the sense of "remove yourselves, lift yourselves away." The identical command appeared in 16:21 before the earth opened. וַאֲכַלֶּה אֹתָם כְּרָגַע (va-akhaleh otam ke-rega) = "and I will consume them in a moment" — the piel cohortative of כלה (kalah, "to complete, finish, consume, destroy") + כְּרָגַע (ke-rega, "like a moment, in an instant"). YHWH threatens instantaneous annihilation of the entire congregation. וַיִּפְּלוּ עַל־פְּנֵיהֶם (va-yippelu al peneihem) = "and they fell upon their faces" — Moses and Aaron's characteristic response: prostration before YHWH, not in submission to judgment but in desperate intercession for the people who have just accused them. **LXX:** ἐκχωρήσατε ἐκ μέσου τῆς συναγωγῆς ταύτης, καὶ ἐξαναλώσω αὐτοὺς εἰσάπαξ ("withdraw from the midst of this congregation, and I will utterly consume them at once").

**11** And-said [va-yomer] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] Aaron [Aharon], "Take [qakh] the fire-pan [et ha-makhtah], and-put [ve-ten] upon-it [aleha] fire [esh] from-upon [me-al] the-altar [ha-mizbeakh], and-place [ve-sim] incense [qetoret], and-go [ve-holekh] quickly [meherah] unto [el] the-congregation [ha-edah], and-make-atonement [ve-khapper] upon-them [aleihem]; for [ki] has-gone-out [yatsa] the-wrath [ha-qetsef] from-before [mi-lifnei] YHWH — has-begun [heikhel] the-plague [ha-negef]!"

> **Key terms:** קַח אֶת־הַמַּחְתָּה (qakh et ha-makhtah) = "take the fire-pan" — the qal imperative of לקח (laqakh, "to take"). The very instrument that brought death to the two hundred fifty rebels now becomes the instrument of life. וְתֶן־עָלֶיהָ אֵשׁ מֵעַל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (ve-ten aleha esh me-al ha-mizbeakh) = "and put upon it fire from upon the altar" — authorized fire from the altar, unlike the "strange fire" of Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:1). The distinction is critical: the fire must come from the altar, the legitimate source of sacred flame. וְשִׂים קְטֹרֶת (ve-sim qetoret) = "and place incense" — the qal imperative of שׂים (sum/sim, "to put, place"). וְהוֹלֵךְ מְהֵרָה (ve-holekh meherah) = "and go quickly" — the qal imperative/active participle of הלך (halakh, "to go, walk") + מְהֵרָה (meherah, "quickly, speedily," from מהר, mahar, "to hurry"). The urgency is palpable — people are dying. וְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיהֶם (ve-khapper aleihem) = "and make atonement upon them" — the piel imperative of כפר (kafar/kaphar, "to cover, atone, make expiation"). This is the first time in the Torah that incense is explicitly used for atonement — incense normally accompanies offerings or rises in the Holy Place, but here it becomes an emergency atoning agent. כִּי־יָצָא הַקֶּצֶף מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה (ki yatsa ha-qetsef mi-lifnei YHWH) = "for the wrath has gone out from before YHWH" — הַקֶּצֶף (ha-qetsef, "the wrath/fury") from קצף (qatsaf, "to be wrathful"). The wrath is personified as a force that "goes out" from YHWH's presence and moves through the camp. הֵחֵל הַנָּגֶף (heikhel ha-nagef) = "the plague has begun" — the hifil perfect of חלל (khalal, "to begin") + הַנָּגֶף (ha-nagef, "the plague/striking") from נגף (nagaf, "to strike, smite"). Moses perceives what is happening before Aaron can see it, and acts immediately. **LXX:** ἐπίθες θυμίαμα καὶ ἄπελθε τὴν ταχίστην εἰς τὴν παρεμβολὴν καὶ ἐξίλασαι περὶ αὐτῶν ("put on incense and go with all speed into the camp and make propitiation concerning them") — ἐξιλάσκομαι ("make propitiation") for ve-khapper; the LXX adds εἰς τὴν παρεμβολήν ("into the camp"), making the destination explicit.

**12** And-took [va-yiqqakh] Aaron [Aharon] as-which [ka-asher] spoke [dibber] Moses [Mosheh], and-he-ran [va-yarats] unto [el] the-midst of [tokh] the-assembly [ha-qahal], and-behold [ve-hinneh], had-begun [heikhel] the-plague [ha-negef] among-the-people [ba-am]; and-he-put [va-yitten] the-incense [et ha-qetoret], and-he-made-atonement [va-yekhapper] upon [al] the-people [ha-am].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּקַּח אַהֲרֹן כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר מֹשֶׁה וַיָּרָץ אֶל־תּוֹךְ הַקָּהָל (va-yiqqakh Aharon ka-asher dibber Mosheh va-yarats el tokh ha-qahal) = "and Aaron took as Moses spoke, and he ran into the midst of the assembly" — the qal vav-consecutive of רוץ (ruts, "to run"). Aaron, the elderly high priest, runs. The verb conveys urgency, self-sacrifice, and the reversal of the priestly protocol that normally forbids the high priest from leaving the sanctuary during service. He runs toward death, not away from it. וְהִנֵּה הֵחֵל הַנֶּגֶף בָּעָם (ve-hinneh heikhel ha-negef ba-am) = "and behold, the plague had begun among the people" — the dramatic הִנֵּה (hinneh) marks Aaron's horrified realization upon arriving. וַיִּתֵּן אֶת־הַקְּטֹרֶת (va-yitten et ha-qetoret) = "and he put the incense" — the qal vav-consecutive of נתן (natan, "to give, put, place"). וַיְכַפֵּר עַל־הָעָם (va-yekhapper al ha-am) = "and he made atonement upon the people" — the piel of כפר. Aaron interposes himself and his incense between YHWH's wrath and the people — the priestly role at its most essential: mediating between divine holiness and human sinfulness. **LXX:** καὶ ἔδραμεν εἰς τὴν συναγωγήν, καὶ ἤδη ἐνῆρκτο ἡ θραῦσις ἐν τῷ λαῷ ("and he ran into the congregation, and already the destruction had begun among the people").

**13** And-he-stood [va-ya'amod] between [bein] the-dead [ha-metim] and-between [u-vein] the-living [ha-khayyim], and-was-restrained [va-te'atsar] the-plague [ha-maggefah].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּעֲמֹד בֵּין־הַמֵּתִים וּבֵין הַחַיִּים (va-ya'amod bein ha-metim u-vein ha-khayyim) = "and he stood between the dead and the living" — the qal vav-consecutive of עמד (amad, "to stand"). This is one of the most powerful images in the entire Torah. Aaron stands — literally, physically — on the boundary between death and life, with the plague advancing on one side and the living retreating on the other. The censer of incense in his hand becomes a barrier that death cannot cross. The high priest as the living boundary between wrath and mercy, between death and life, between divine holiness and human frailty. הַמֵּתִים (ha-metim, "the dead") — the qal active participle of מות (mut, "to die"), used substantively: those who have died / are dying. הַחַיִּים (ha-khayyim, "the living") — from חי (khai, "alive, living"). וַתֵּעָצַר הַמַּגֵּפָה (va-te'atsar ha-maggefah) = "and the plague was restrained" — the niphal of עצר (atsar, "to restrain, hold back, stop"). הַמַּגֵּפָה (ha-maggefah, "the plague/striking") from נגף (nagaf, "to strike") — a synonym of נֶגֶף (negef) used in vv. 11-12. The plague stops. Aaron's priestly intercession succeeds where human argument failed. **LXX:** καὶ ἔστη ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν τεθνηκότων καὶ τῶν ζώντων, καὶ ἐκόπασεν ἡ θραῦσις ("and he stood in the midst between the dead and the living, and the destruction ceased") — κοπάζω ("cease, abate") for va-te'atsar.

**14** And-were [va-yihyu] the-dead [ha-metim] by-the-plague [ba-maggefah] — four-ten [arba'ah asar] thousand [elef] and-seven [u-sheva] hundreds [me'ot], apart-from [mi-levad] the-dead [ha-metim] on-the-matter of [al devar] Korah [Qorakh].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּהְיוּ הַמֵּתִים בַּמַּגֵּפָה אַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר אֶלֶף וּשְׁבַע מֵאוֹת (va-yihyu ha-metim ba-maggefah arba'ah asar elef u-sheva me'ot) = "and the dead by the plague were fourteen thousand and seven hundred" — fourteen thousand seven hundred dead in addition to those who perished in the Korah rebellion proper. The number is devastating — far exceeding the two hundred fifty incense-offerers and the households of Dathan and Abiram. מִלְּבַד הַמֵּתִים עַל־דְּבַר־קֹרַח (mi-levad ha-metim al devar Qorakh) = "apart from the dead on the matter of Korah" — מִלְּבַד (mi-levad, "apart from, besides") distinguishes this plague's casualties from those who died in the previous chapter's judgments. עַל־דְּבַר (al devar, "on the matter of, on account of, concerning") — from דָּבָר (davar, "word, matter, thing"). **LXX:** καὶ ἐγένοντο οἱ τεθνηκότες ἐν τῇ θραύσει τεσσαρεσκαίδεκα χιλιάδες καὶ ἑπτακόσιοι, χωρὶς τῶν τεθνηκότων ἕνεκεν Κορε ("and the dead in the destruction were fourteen thousand and seven hundred, apart from the dead on account of Korah").

**15** And-returned [va-yashav] Aaron [Aharon] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], unto [el] the-entrance of [petakh] the-Tent of [ohel] Meeting [mo'ed], and-the-plague [ve-ha-maggefah] was-restrained [ne'etsarah].

> **Key terms:** וַיָּשָׁב אַהֲרֹן אֶל־מֹשֶׁה אֶל־פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (va-yashav Aharon el Mosheh el petakh ohel mo'ed) = "and Aaron returned to Moses, to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting" — the qal vav-consecutive of שׁוב (shuv, "to return"). Aaron's mission of intercession is complete; he returns to the sanctuary entrance where Moses waits. וְהַמַּגֵּפָה נֶעֱצָרָה (ve-ha-maggefah ne'etsarah) = "and the plague was restrained" — the niphal perfect of עצר (atsar, "to restrain"), repeating the statement of v. 13 with a concluding finality. The narrative comes to rest: the plague is stopped, Aaron has returned, the crisis is over — but the underlying challenge to priestly authority remains unresolved. The next section (vv. 16-28) will address that definitively. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπέστρεψεν Ααρων πρὸς Μωυσῆν ἐπὶ τὴν θύραν τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου, καὶ ἐκόπασεν ἡ θραῦσις ("and Aaron returned to Moses at the door of the tent of the testimony, and the destruction ceased").

---

## The Sign of Aaron's Rod (vv. 16-28)

**16** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor) = "and YHWH spoke to Moses, saying" — the third occurrence of this formula in the chapter (vv. 1, 9, 16), marking a new divine initiative. After judgment by fire (ch. 16), by earth (16:31-33), and by plague (17:6-15), YHWH now turns to a sign of life rather than death. **LXX:** καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων ("and the Lord spoke to Moses, saying"). **Versification note:** This verse corresponds to Numbers 17:1 in English translations that begin chapter 17 here.

**17** "Speak [dabber] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-take [ve-qakh] from-them [me-ittam] a-rod [matteh], a-rod [matteh] for-a-father's-house [le-veit av], from [me-et] all [kol] their-leaders [nesi'eihem], for-the-house of [le-veit] their-fathers [avotam] — twelve [shenim asar] rods [mattot]; each-man's [ish] name [et shemo] you-shall-write [tikhtov] upon [al] his-rod [mattehu].

> **Key terms:** דַּבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְקַח מֵאִתָּם מַטֶּה מַטֶּה לְבֵית אָב (dabber el benei Yisra'el ve-qakh me-ittam matteh matteh le-veit av) = "speak to the Sons of Israel and take from them a rod, a rod for a father's house" — the repeated מַטֶּה מַטֶּה (matteh matteh) is distributive: "one rod per father's house." מַטֶּה (matteh) means both "rod/staff" and "tribe" (the staff being the symbol of tribal authority). The double meaning is intentional: the tribal leaders present their tribal symbol — the rod of authority. לְבֵית אָב (le-veit av, "for a father's house") = a patriarchal household unit. מֵאֵת כָּל־נְשִׂיאֵיהֶם (me-et kol nesi'eihem) = "from all their leaders" — each נָשִׂיא (nasi, "prince/chief/leader") represents one tribe. שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר מַטּוֹת (shenim asar mattot) = "twelve rods" — one per tribe. אִישׁ אֶת־שְׁמוֹ תִּכְתֹּב עַל־מַטֵּהוּ (ish et shemo tikhtov al mattehu) = "each man's name you shall write upon his rod" — the qal imperfect of כתב (katav, "to write"). The rods are personalized — inscribed with the leader's name, linking identity to authority. **LXX:** λάβε παρ᾽ αὐτῶν ῥάβδον ῥάβδον κατ᾽ οἴκους πατριῶν ("take from them a rod, a rod according to houses of clans") — ῥάβδος (rhabdos, "rod/staff") for matteh.

**18** And [ve-et] the-name of [shem] Aaron [Aharon] you-shall-write [tikhtov] upon [al] the-rod of [matteh] Levi [Levi]; for [ki] one [ekhad] rod [matteh] for-the-head of [le-rosh] the-house of [beit] their-fathers [avotam].

> **Key terms:** וְאֵת שֵׁם אַהֲרֹן תִּכְתֹּב עַל־מַטֵּה לֵוִי (ve-et shem Aharon tikhtov al matteh Levi) = "and the name of Aaron you shall write upon the rod of Levi" — Aaron represents the tribe of Levi. His name, not Moses's, appears on the Levitical rod because the dispute is specifically about the priesthood, not about civil leadership. כִּי מַטֶּה אֶחָד לְרֹאשׁ בֵּית אֲבוֹתָם (ki matteh ekhad le-rosh beit avotam) = "for one rod for the head of the house of their fathers" — each tribe gets exactly one rod; Levi is not given an extra one. Aaron's rod is the Levitical tribal rod, not an additional thirteenth rod. This is important: the test is between the twelve tribes, with Levi as one of the twelve, not a supplementary entry. **LXX:** ὅτι ῥάβδος μία ἐστίν· κατὰ φυλὴν οἴκου πατριᾶς ἔσονται ("for there is one rod; according to the tribe of a father's house they shall be") — **LXX variant:** the Greek restructures to emphasize the one-rod-per-tribe principle.

**19** And-you-shall-lay-them [ve-hinnakhtam] in-the-Tent of [be-ohel] Meeting [mo'ed], before [lifnei] the-Testimony [ha-edut], where [asher] I-meet [ivvaled] with-you [lakhem] there [shammah].

> **Key terms:** וְהִנַּחְתָּם בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לִפְנֵי הָעֵדוּת (ve-hinnakhtam be-ohel mo'ed lifnei ha-edut) = "and you shall lay them in the Tent of Meeting before the Testimony" — the hifil perfect 2ms + 3mp suffix of נוח (nuakh, "to rest, lay down") with vav-consecutive. The rods are placed inside the Tent, before הָעֵדוּת (ha-edut, "the Testimony") — i.e., the Ark of the Covenant containing the tablets of the law. The rods are laid in the most sacred space in Israel, directly before the seat of divine presence. אֲשֶׁר אִוָּעֵד לָכֶם שָׁמָּה (asher ivvaled lakhem shammah) = "where I meet with you there" — the niphal imperfect of יעד (ya'ad, "to appoint, meet by appointment"). The Tent of Meeting is so named because it is the place of divine-human appointment — YHWH meets (יִוָּעֵד, yivva'ed) Israel there. The reflexive niphal suggests mutuality: God "causes Himself to meet" with His people. **LXX:** καὶ θήσεις αὐτὰς ἐν τῇ σκηνῇ τοῦ μαρτυρίου κατέναντι τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and you shall place them in the tent of the testimony, opposite the testimony").

**20** And-it-shall-be [ve-hayah], the-man [ha-ish] whom [asher] I-shall-choose [evkhar] in-him [bo] — his-rod [mattehu] will-bud [yifrakh]; and-I-will-cause-to-subside [va-hashikkoti] from-upon-me [me-alai] the-murmurings of [et telunnot] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] which [asher] they [hem] murmur [mallinim] against-you [aleikem]."

> **Key terms:** וְהָיָה הָאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר אֶבְחַר בּוֹ מַטֵּהוּ יִפְרָח (ve-hayah ha-ish asher evkhar bo mattehu yifrakh) = "and it shall be, the man whom I shall choose in him — his rod will bud" — אֶבְחַר (evkhar) = qal imperfect 1cs of בחר (bakhar, "to choose, elect"). The verb of divine election — the same verb used of YHWH choosing Israel (Deut 7:6), choosing Jerusalem (1 Kgs 8:44), choosing David (Ps 78:70). יִפְרָח (yifrakh) = qal imperfect 3ms of פרח (parakh, "to bud, sprout, blossom, flourish"). A dead, dry rod will produce living growth — the miracle will be unmistakable. The sign is one of life, not death: after all the killing and plague, YHWH demonstrates His choice through a sign of creation, fertility, and vitality. וַהֲשִׁכֹּתִי מֵעָלַי אֶת־תְּלֻנֹּת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (va-hashikkoti me-alai et telunnot benei Yisra'el) = "and I will cause to subside from upon me the murmurings of the Sons of Israel" — the hifil perfect of שׁכך (shakhakh, "to subside, abate, be assuaged") with vav-consecutive. תְּלֻנֹּת (telunnot, "murmurings/grumblings") from לון (lun, "to murmur"). YHWH speaks of the murmurings as being מֵעָלַי (me-alai, "from upon me") — the complaints weigh upon YHWH Himself; they are not merely human social unrest but an offense directed at God. אֲשֶׁר הֵם מַלִּינִם עֲלֵיכֶם (asher hem mallinim aleikem) = "which they are murmuring against you" — the hifil participle of לין/לון, ongoing continuous murmuring. **LXX:** καὶ ἔσται ὁ ἄνθρωπος ὃν ἐκλέξομαι αὐτόν, ἡ ῥάβδος αὐτοῦ ἐκβλαστήσει ("and it shall be, the man whom I shall choose, his rod will sprout") — ἐκβλαστάνω ("sprout forth") for yifrakh.

**21** And-spoke [va-yedabber] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-gave [va-yittenu] unto-him [elav] all [kol] their-leaders [nesi'eihem], a-rod [matteh] for-one [le-ekhad] leader [nasi], a-rod [matteh] for-one [le-ekhad] leader [nasi], for-the-house of [le-veit] their-fathers [avotam] — twelve [shenim asar] rods [mattot]; and-the-rod of [u-matteh] Aaron [Aharon] [was] in-the-midst of [be-tokh] their-rods [mattotam].

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיִּתְּנוּ אֵלָיו כָּל־נְשִׂיאֵיהֶם מַטֶּה לְאֶחָד נָשִׂיא מַטֶּה לְאֶחָד נָשִׂיא לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר מַטּוֹת (va-yedabber Mosheh el benei Yisra'el va-yittenu elav kol nesi'eihem matteh le-ekhad nasi matteh le-ekhad nasi le-veit avotam shenim asar mattot) = "and Moses spoke to the Sons of Israel, and all their leaders gave to him — a rod for one leader, a rod for one leader, for the house of their fathers — twelve rods." The repetition מַטֶּה לְאֶחָד נָשִׂיא מַטֶּה לְאֶחָד נָשִׂיא (matteh le-ekhad nasi matteh le-ekhad nasi) is distributive: "one rod per leader, one rod per leader." וּמַטֵּה אַהֲרֹן בְּתוֹךְ מַטּוֹתָם (u-matteh Aharon be-tokh mattotam) = "and the rod of Aaron was in the midst of their rods" — Aaron's rod was placed among the others, not set apart or given any visible advantage. The test is fair: all twelve rods are treated identically. **LXX:** καὶ ἔδωκαν αὐτῷ πάντες οἱ ἄρχοντες αὐτῶν ῥάβδον, τῷ ἄρχοντι τῷ ἑνὶ ῥάβδον κατ᾽ οἴκον πατριᾶς ("and all their rulers gave him a rod, one rod per ruler according to the house of the clan").

**22** And-laid [va-yannakh] Moses [Mosheh] the-rods [et ha-mattot] before [lifnei] YHWH in-the-Tent of [be-ohel] the-Testimony [ha-edut].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּנַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת־הַמַּטֹּת לִפְנֵי יְהוָה בְּאֹהֶל הָעֵדֻת (va-yannakh Mosheh et ha-mattot lifnei YHWH be-ohel ha-edut) = "and Moses laid the rods before YHWH in the Tent of the Testimony" — the hifil vav-consecutive of נוח (nuakh, "to rest, lay down"). Note that here the Tabernacle is called אֹהֶל הָעֵדֻת (ohel ha-edut, "Tent of the Testimony") rather than the more common אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed, "Tent of Meeting") — emphasizing the rods' placement near the Ark containing the tablets of testimony. **LXX:** καὶ ἀπέθηκεν Μωυσῆς τὰς ῥάβδους ἔναντι κυρίου ἐν τῇ σκηνῇ τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and Moses laid the rods before the Lord in the tent of the testimony").

**23** And-it-was [va-yehi] on-the-next-day [mi-mokhorat], and-came [va-yavo] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] the-Tent of [ohel] the-Testimony [ha-edut], and-behold [ve-hinneh], had-budded [parakh] the-rod of [matteh] Aaron [Aharon] for-the-house of [le-veit] Levi [Levi]; and-it-brought-forth [va-yotse] buds [perakh], and-it-blossomed [va-yatsets] blossoms [tsits], and-it-ripened [va-yigmol] almonds [sheqedim].

> **Key terms:** וַיְהִי מִמָּחֳרָת וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אֹהֶל הָעֵדוּת (va-yehi mi-mokhorat va-yavo Mosheh el ohel ha-edut) = "and it was on the next day, and Moses came to the Tent of the Testimony" — a single night has passed. The miracle occurs in darkness, in the most sacred space, unseen by any human eye. וְהִנֵּה פָּרַח מַטֵּה־אַהֲרֹן לְבֵית לֵוִי (ve-hinneh parakh matteh Aharon le-veit Levi) = "and behold, the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi had budded" — the dramatic הִנֵּה (hinneh, "behold!") signals Moses's astonishment. פָּרַח (parakh) = qal perfect of פרח (parakh, "to bud, sprout, blossom") — the very verb YHWH predicted in v. 20. וַיֹּצֵא פֶרַח (va-yotse perakh) = "and it brought forth buds" — the hifil vav-consecutive of יצא (yatsa, "to go out") + פֶּרַח (perakh, "bud, blossom, flower"). The rod "caused buds to go out" — active, creative production from dead wood. וַיָּצֵץ צִיץ (va-yatsets tsits) = "and it blossomed blossoms" — the hifil of צוץ (tsuts, "to blossom, flower") + צִיץ (tsits, "blossom, flower") — cognate accusative construction: "it blossomed a blossoming." וַיִּגְמֹל שְׁקֵדִים (va-yigmol sheqedim) = "and it ripened almonds" — the qal vav-consecutive of גמל (gamal, "to ripen, wean, deal fully with") + שְׁקֵדִים (sheqedim, "almonds," plural of שָׁקֵד, shaqed). The rod displays three simultaneous stages of growth that in nature are sequential: buds (earliest), blossoms (middle), and ripe fruit (final). The entire life-cycle of a tree — from first stirring to mature fruit — is compressed into a single night. שָׁקֵד (shaqed, "almond") is from the root שׁקד (shaqad, "to watch, be alert, be wakeful"). The almond tree (שְׁקֵדִיָּה, sheqediyyah) is called the "watcher" because it is the first tree to blossom in early spring, as if "watching" for the end of winter. Jeremiah's wordplay is foundational: "What do you see, Yirmeyahu?" "A rod of almond (maqqel shaqed)." "You have seen well, for I am watching (shoqed) over my word to perform it" (Jer 1:11-12). YHWH is "watching" over Aaron's rod just as He "watches" over His word. **LXX:** καὶ ἐγένετο τῇ ἐπαύριον, καὶ εἰσῆλθεν Μωυσῆς εἰς τὴν σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου, καὶ ἰδοὺ ἐβλάστησεν ἡ ῥάβδος Ααρων εἰς οἶκον Λευι, καὶ ἐξήνεγκεν βλαστὸν καὶ ἐξήνθησεν ἄνθη καὶ ἐβλάστησεν κάρυα ("and it was on the morrow, and Moses entered the tent of the testimony, and behold, the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi had sprouted, and it brought forth a sprout, and it blossomed blossoms, and it produced nuts") — **LXX variant:** κάρυα ("nuts") for sheqedim ("almonds") — the LXX uses a more generic term for the fruit.

**24** And-brought-out [va-yotse] Moses [Mosheh] all [et kol] the-rods [ha-mattot] from-before [mi-lifnei] YHWH unto [el] all [kol] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-they-saw [va-yir'u], and-they-took [va-yiqkhu] each-man [ish] his-rod [mattehu].

> **Key terms:** וַיֹּצֵא מֹשֶׁה אֶת־כָּל־הַמַּטֹּת מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה אֶל־כָּל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (va-yotse Mosheh et kol ha-mattot mi-lifnei YHWH el kol benei Yisra'el) = "and Moses brought out all the rods from before YHWH to all the Sons of Israel" — Moses brings all twelve rods out publicly so the entire nation can witness the result. The test was conducted in the most sacred space (before YHWH); the results are displayed in the most public space (before all Israel). וַיִּרְאוּ וַיִּקְחוּ אִישׁ מַטֵּהוּ (va-yir'u va-yiqkhu ish mattehu) = "and they saw, and they took each man his rod" — the qal vav-consecutive of ראה (ra'ah, "to see") followed by the qal of לקח (laqakh, "to take"). The leaders see the evidence and reclaim their rods — eleven unchanged dead sticks, and one miraculously alive. The seeing precedes the taking: the evidence is examined before any rod is removed. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξήνεγκεν Μωυσῆς πάσας τὰς ῥάβδους ἀπὸ προσώπου κυρίου πρὸς πάντας υἱοὺς Ισραηλ, καὶ εἶδον καὶ ἔλαβον ἕκαστος τὴν ῥάβδον αὐτοῦ ("and Moses brought out all the rods from the face of the Lord to all the sons of Israel, and they saw and each took his rod").

**25** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], "Return [hashev] the-rod of [et matteh] Aaron [Aharon] before [lifnei] the-Testimony [ha-edut], for-keeping [le-mishmeret] for-a-sign [le-ot] for-the-sons of [li-vnei] rebellion [meri], and-you-shall-finish [u-tekhol] their-murmurings [telunnotam] from-upon-me [me-alai], and-not [ve-lo] they-shall-die [yamutu]."

> **Key terms:** הָשֵׁב אֶת־מַטֵּה אַהֲרֹן לִפְנֵי הָעֵדוּת (hashev et matteh Aharon lifnei ha-edut) = "return the rod of Aaron before the Testimony" — the hifil imperative of שׁוב (shuv, "to return"). Aaron's rod is to be placed permanently before the Ark — alongside (according to later tradition, inside) the Ark with the tablets of testimony and the jar of manna (cf. Heb 9:4). לְמִשְׁמֶרֶת (le-mishmeret) = "for keeping, for guarding, for safekeeping" — from שׁמר (shamar, "to keep, guard"). The rod is preserved as a perpetual artifact. לְאוֹת (le-ot) = "for a sign" — as in v. 3, but now the sign is one of life and election, not of death and warning. לִבְנֵי־מֶרִי (li-vnei meri) = "for the sons of rebellion" — from מֶרִי (meri, "rebellion, contrariness") from מרה (marah, "to be rebellious/contentious"). The phrase בְּנֵי מֶרִי (benei meri) characterizes those who habitually rebel — a stinging epithet for the murmuring Israelites. וּתְכַל תְּלוּנֹּתָם מֵעָלַי (u-tekhol telunnotam me-alai) = "and you shall finish their murmurings from upon me" — the piel imperfect of כלה (kalah, "to finish, end, complete") with vav-consecutive. The rod's permanent presence should put an end to the complaints that "weigh upon" (מֵעָלַי, me-alai) YHWH. וְלֹא יָמֻתוּ (ve-lo yamutu) = "and they shall not die" — the qal imperfect of מות (mut, "to die"). The purpose of the sign is ultimately merciful: by ending the murmuring, it prevents the further deaths that murmuring provokes. **LXX:** ἀπόθες τὴν ῥάβδον Ααρων ἐνώπιον τῶν μαρτυρίων εἰς διατήρησιν, σημεῖον τοῖς υἱοῖς τῶν ἀνηκόων ("lay the rod of Aaron before the testimonies for preservation, a sign for the sons of the disobedient") — ἀνήκοος ("disobedient, heedless") for benei meri.

**26** And-did [va-ya'as] Moses [Mosheh]; as [ka-asher] commanded [tsivvah] YHWH [him], so [ken] he-did [asah].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּעַשׂ מֹשֶׁה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֹתוֹ כֵּן עָשָׂה (va-ya'as Mosheh ka-asher tsivvah YHWH oto ken asah) = "and Moses did; as YHWH commanded him, so he did" — the formulaic obedience conclusion. The qal vav-consecutive of עשׂה (asah, "to do, make") repeated: "he did... so he did." The double statement emphasizes completeness of compliance. **LXX:** καὶ ἐποίησεν Μωυσῆς καθὰ συνέταξεν κύριος αὐτῷ, οὕτως ἐποίησεν ("and Moses did as the Lord commanded him, so he did").

**27** And-said [va-yomru] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor], "Behold [hen], we-have-expired [gavanu], we-have-perished [avadnu], all-of-us [kullanu] have-perished [avadnu]!

> **Key terms:** וַיֹּאמְרוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵאמֹר הֵן גָּוַעְנוּ אָבַדְנוּ כֻּלָּנוּ אָבָדְנוּ (va-yomru benei Yisra'el el Mosheh le'mor hen gavanu avadnu kullanu avadnu) = "and the Sons of Israel said to Moses, saying, 'Behold, we have expired, we have perished, all of us have perished!'" — הֵן (hen, "behold!") introduces an exclamation of despair. גָּוַעְנוּ (gavanu) = qal perfect 1cp of גוע (gava, "to expire, breathe one's last, die"). The verb gava specifically denotes the moment of death — the final breath, the expiration. אָבַדְנוּ (avadnu) = qal perfect 1cp of אבד (avad, "to perish, be lost, be destroyed"), repeated twice: "we have perished... we have perished!" The triple cascade — gavanu, avadnu, kullanu avadnu ("we have expired, we have perished, all of us have perished!") — is a crescendo of panic. The nation now swings from rebellious arrogance to abject terror. Having seen plague kill fourteen thousand seven hundred, having seen fire consume two hundred fifty, having seen the earth swallow entire families, they conclude that all of them are doomed. The rhetorical structure — three verbs of death in rapid succession — conveys collective hysteria. **LXX:** ἰδοὺ ἐξανηλώμεθα, ἀπολώλαμεν, παραναλώμεθα ("behold, we are consumed, we have perished, we are utterly destroyed") — the LXX uses three different verbs of destruction to match the Hebrew's triple death-cry.

**28** Every-one [kol] who-comes-near [ha-qarev], the-one-coming-near [ha-qarev] unto [el] the-Tabernacle of [mishkan] YHWH, will-die [yamut]. Have-we-finished [ha-im tamnu] dying [ligvo'a]?"

> **Key terms:** כֹּל הַקָּרֵב הַקָּרֵב אֶל־מִשְׁכַּן יְהוָה יָמוּת (kol ha-qarev ha-qarev el mishkan YHWH yamut) = "every one who comes near — the one coming near — to the Tabernacle of YHWH, will die" — the doubled participle הַקָּרֵב הַקָּרֵב (ha-qarev ha-qarev, "the one coming near, the one coming near") intensifies the statement: anyone at all who approaches. The qal active participle of קרב (qarav, "to come near, approach"), the same root that is the technical term for priestly approach and for offering sacrifices (הַקְרָבָה, haqravah; קָרְבָּן, qorban). The people now fear that any approach to the mishkan is lethal — the very act that is meant to sustain the covenant (sacrificial worship) has become, in their terrified perception, a death sentence. הַאִם תַּמְנוּ לִגְוֹעַ (ha-im tamnu ligvo'a) = "have we finished dying?" — the interrogative הַאִם (ha-im, "have we?") + qal perfect 1cp of תמם (tamam, "to be complete, finished, ended") + the qal infinitive construct of גוע (gava, "to expire, die"). The despairing question: "Is the dying over, or will it continue until none of us remain?" This cry of anguish forms the thematic bridge to chapter 18, where YHWH responds by delineating the precise roles of priests and Levites — establishing who may approach the sanctuary and how, thus providing the institutional answer to the people's existential terror. The priestly system is not an arbitrary hierarchy but the divinely ordained mechanism that allows sinful humanity to draw near to a holy God without dying. **LXX:** πᾶς ὁ ἁπτόμενος τῆς σκηνῆς κυρίου ἀποθνήσκει· εἰ συντελέσθαι ἡμᾶς ἀποθνήσκοντας ("everyone who touches the tent of the Lord dies; are we to be finished off, dying?") — **LXX variant:** ἅπτομαι ("touch") for qarev ("come near") — the LXX intensifies the fear by using "touch" rather than "approach," suggesting an even more expansive danger zone.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Versification
The chapter numbering and verse numbering of Numbers 17 differ significantly between the Hebrew Masoretic tradition and most English translations. The Hebrew chapter 17 contains twenty-eight verses:
- **MT 17:1-15** = English 16:36-50 in many translations (ESV, NIV, NASB, NRSV)
- **MT 17:16-28** = English 17:1-13 in the same translations
- The LXX and Vulgate numbering also differs from the MT; the Vaticanus manuscript transitions between its chapters XIX-XX across these verses

The EMET follows the Masoretic Text verse numbering throughout.

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (Pages 169-170)
The text of Numbers 17 on pages 169-170 of the Leningrad Codex (B19A) is written in the standard three-column format. Page 169 (folio 85r) contains the end of chapter 16 and the beginning of chapter 17; page 170 (folio 85v, marked "86" in the upper margin) continues through chapter 17 and into chapter 18. The chapter division is marked with a parashah setumah (closed section) break. The Masoretic notes in the margins include verse counts and cross-references. The text is clearly legible with standard Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks.

### Manuscript Observations from Codex Vaticanus (pa_0125-0126)
The Vaticanus text (Vat.gr.1209) on pages 125-126 presents the LXX Greek in continuous uncial majuscule script without word separation, in three columns per page. The text of Numbers 17 spans the transition from page 125 to page 126. The chapter heading "CAP. XX" is visible at the top of page 125 (corresponding to the LXX numbering). No significant lacunae or corrections are visible in this section.

### Key Textual Variants

1. **v. 2 — "scatter" (זְרֵה, zereh):** The MT uses the qal imperative of זרה (zarah, "to scatter/winnow"), addressed to Eleazar. Some commentators suggest the address shifts mid-verse from Eleazar (who lifts the pans) to an unnamed assistant (who scatters the fire), based on the change from third-person ("let him lift") to second-person ("scatter").

2. **v. 3 — "sinners against their souls" (הַחַטָּאִים בְּנַפְשֹׁתָם):** The phrase be-nafshosam is interpreted variously: (a) "against their [own] souls/lives" — their sin cost them their lives; (b) "at the cost of their lives"; (c) the Samaritan Pentateuch and some versions read slightly differently. The LXX renders τῶν ἁμαρτωλῶν τούτων ἐν ταῖς ψυχαῖς αὐτῶν ("of these sinners in/with their souls").

3. **v. 5 — "to him" (לוֹ, lo):** The final pronoun is ambiguous in its antecedent. It could refer to: (a) Eleazar — YHWH spoke through Moses to Eleazar; (b) the stranger (zar) — as a warning directed at any unauthorized person; (c) Korah — as YHWH spoke to Korah through Moses. Most interpreters favor (a).

4. **v. 13 — "between the dead and the living":** This verse is identical in MT and LXX in substance, though the LXX uses τεθνηκότων (perfect participle, "those who have died") and ζώντων (present participle, "the living"), preserving the dramatic temporal contrast.

5. **v. 23 — "almonds" (שְׁקֵדִים, sheqedim) vs. LXX "nuts" (κάρυα, karya):** The LXX renders the specific Hebrew "almonds" with the generic Greek "nuts." This may reflect a different Vorlage, a translation choice due to Greek botanical terminology, or uncertainty about the exact species. The Hebrew שָׁקֵד (shaqed) is unambiguously the almond (Prunus dulcis / Amygdalus communis), native to the Levant and one of the earliest cultivated nut trees.

6. **v. 28 — "come near" (הַקָּרֵב, ha-qarev) vs. LXX "touch" (ὁ ἁπτόμενος, ho haptomenos):** The LXX intensifies the people's fear by rendering "approach" as "touch," suggesting an even more expansive zone of lethal holiness around the Tabernacle. The MT's qarev ("draw near") is the standard priestly approach term.

### Theological Structure of the Chapter

The three sections of Numbers 17 form a progression from death to life:

| Section | Content | Mode of Divine Action | Result |
|---------|---------|----------------------|--------|
| vv. 1-5 | Fire-pans hammered into altar | Memorial of judgment | Warning sign (אוֹת) |
| vv. 6-15 | Plague and Aaron's intercession | Wrath restrained by priestly mediation | 14,700 dead; plague stopped |
| vv. 16-28 | Aaron's rod buds | Sign of divine election through life | Living sign (אוֹת) of chosen priesthood |

The movement from fire-pan memorial (death-artifact) to budding rod (life-artifact) mirrors the Torah's broader theology: judgment is real but not God's final word; life and election emerge from the aftermath of death and rebellion. Aaron, who stood between the dead and the living with his censer (v. 13), is now confirmed as YHWH's chosen priest through a rod that brings life from death — a dead stick that sprouts, blossoms, and bears fruit.

### The Rod's Later Tradition
According to Hebrews 9:4, "the ark of the covenant" contained "the golden pot that had manna, and Aaron's rod that budded, and the tablets of the covenant." The rod thus joins the manna and the tablets as one of three artifacts preserved inside (or before) the Ark — three witnesses to YHWH's provision (manna), YHWH's law (tablets), and YHWH's chosen mediator (rod). In rabbinic tradition, Aaron's rod was among the ten things created at twilight on the eve of the first Sabbath (Pirkei Avot 5:6).
